content.problemStatement:
You are given a sequence of positive integers a1,a2,…,aNa_1, a_2, \ldots, a_Na1​,a2​,…,aN​. You may change up to two elements of this sequence, each of them to any positive integer (not necessarily the same for two elements). Let's denote the resulting sequence by b1,b2,…,bNb_1, b_2, \ldots, b_Nb1​,b2​,…,bN​.

Consider all 2N2^N2N subsequences of b1,b2,…,bNb_1, b_2, \ldots, b_Nb1​,b2​,…,bN​. For each subsequence, compute the sum of its elements (000 for the empty subsequence). Let's denote the set of these sums by SSS; note that a set contains each integer at most once. Finally, NOSS is the size of set SSS. For example, if [b1,b2,b3]=[17,23,40][b_1, b_2, b_3] = [17, 23, 40][b1​,b2​,b3​]=[17,23,40], then S={0,17,23,40,57,63,80}S = \{0, 17, 23, 40, 57, 63, 80\}S={0,17,23,40,57,63,80} and NOSS is 777.

Find the maximum possible NOSS.
content.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains a single integer T denoting the number of test cases. The description of T test cases follows. The first line of each test case contains a single integer N. The second line contains N space-separated integers a1, a2, …, aN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one integer — the maximum possible NOSS.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Example case 1: One possible way is to change 3 to 10 and 5 to 20. Then S={0…7,10…17,20…27,30…37} and NOSS is 32.
Example case 2: One possible way is to change one element of the initial sequence from 1 to 10 and another from 1 to 20.
Example case 3: The optimal NOSS is achieved even if nothing is changed.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a dynamic programming approach. We can generate all possible subsequences of the given sequence and calculate the sum of each subsequence. We can use bit manipulation to generate all possible subsequences. We can then use a set to store the unique sums of the subsequences. Finally, we can return the size of the set as the maximum possible NOSS.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * 2^N * N) — explain: per query is O(2^N * N), total is O(T * 2^N * N)
